text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon dissecting the angular incisure, the angle between the esophagus and the proximal stomach, as part of the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon dissects the lesser curvature of the stomach and creates an opening between the lesser curvature and the lesser omentum, visually gaining entry into the lesser sac.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to construct a five to six centimeter gastric pouch off the lesser curvature of the stomach, with the pouch formed around an Evo tube. The final staple is fired to complete the pouch construction.
The surgeon examines the ligament of Treitz, with the inferior mesenteric vein visible nearby, while measuring approximately forty centimeters along the small bowel.

The surgeon uses a white load stapler to transect the small bowel. On the right side of the screen, the biliopancreatic limb is visible, while the roux limb is on the left. A clip is placed on the biliopancreatic limb for identification.
The surgeon brings the newly formed limb up to the gastric pouch and begins constructing a two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis using micro-stitches.
The surgeon ties the first stitch of the anastomosis, beginning to run the first layer of sutures along the extension.

The surgeon uses a manic scalpel to create an opening in the gastric pouch, approximately 1.5 centimeters in size. Subsequently, an opening is made in the roux limb, slightly smaller to account for stretching.

The surgeon constructs the second layer of the anastomosis using two micro stitches, carefully securing the corners with separate bites for precision. The stitches encompass full thickness layers of both the small bowel and gastric pouch.

The surgeon stops stitching just before reaching the corner of the anastomosis and begins a new stitch on the right side. Approximately four separate stitches are used for this anastomosis. The EO-II is passed from the gastric pouch into the small bowel.

The surgeon completes the anterior wall of the anastomosis using gaiter stitches, employing two micro stitches to finalize the last layer of the hand-sewn anastomosis between the gastric pouch and the small bowel.

The surgeon tests the anastomosis by injecting air and submerging it under water to check for leaks. Subsequently, the surgeon measures and runs the Roux limb for approximately 100 to 150 centimeters to construct the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon places stay stitches between the Roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb, followed by creating enterotomies in each limb. The anastomosis is constructed using a 60mm stapler with a white load.

The surgeon closes the resulting defect in two layers using ovacro stitches, focusing on the area around the stitches. Once complete, the mesenteric defect is closed using two or six sutures, with separate four-stroke sutures applied to ensure precision.
The completed jugular jejunostomy is visible, with silk sutures used to close a pituitous defect.
